feature_name: Dual-mode operation (RC or EP, legacy/native)
description: The PCIe core integrates the Transaction, Data Link, and MAC layers and can operate as a Root Complex or as an Endpoint. It supports both legacy endpoint and native PCIe endpoint modes selectable via PCIESSMODE[1:0] bootstraps. Software may override the bootstrapped mode after power-up by writing the mode control bits in the device status register. This flexibility allows the same silicon to attach upstream to a PCIe fabric or downstream to a host. Mode changes require appropriate reset and re-enumeration sequencing to take effect.
dependencies: PCIESSMODE[1:0] straps; software override register; reset/re-enumeration
Remarks: Select mode to match system topology and lock it before enabling link training.

feature_name: PCIe PHY SerDes with PLL/CDR/scrambler and test logic
description: The PCIe PHY provides the analog SerDes including PLL, transceivers, clock/data recovery, serializers/deserializers, scrambler, and test/config logic. It implements the PIPE interface toward the core for link training and data movement. The block requires a clean differential reference clock and proper on-die/external terminations. Configuration of PHY parameters is performed through control registers. Stable clocking and proper lane enablement are prerequisites for reliable operation.
dependencies: PCIECLKP/PCIECLKN; PHY control registers; board terminations
Remarks: Program PHY/PLL settings before enabling LTSSM and verify clock quality margins.

feature_name: 128-bit VBUSM master/slave interfaces for DMA and MMR
description: The subsystem exposes one 128-bit VBUSM master port for inbound DMA transfers to the CPU side. It also exposes one 128-bit VBUSM slave port used for PCIe module MMR access and outbound data movement. This split interface enables concurrent configuration and data traffic. Drivers orchestrate DMA by configuring translation windows and buffer addresses. The architecture is designed for high-throughput memory accesses across the PCIe fabric.
dependencies: VBUSM interconnect; translation windows; buffer memory
Remarks: Align buffers to bus width and maintain cache coherency across PCIe transactions.

feature_name: Hardware inbound/outbound address translation
description: Built-in translators map internal bus addresses to PCIe addresses and vice versa based on TLP Type routing. Both outbound and inbound translations are supported to bridge between local address space and PCIe Memory/IO/Configuration/Message spaces. Messages do not consume Memory or I/O resources and are handled accordingly. I/O addressing is for legacy capability and may be omitted in deployments. Proper setup of translation entries is required before enabling bus mastering.
dependencies: Translation configuration registers; BAR/windows; RC enumeration
Remarks: Define non-overlapping windows and prefer Memory space over legacy I/O for portability.

feature_name: Single PCIe link with x1 or x2 lane configuration
description: The PCIe subsystem provides a single link that can be configured for either ×1 or ×2 lane arrangements. It does not support connecting to two devices as separate ×1 links. The chosen width must match the board routing and remote device capability. Link training negotiates the effective width based on both ends. Differential pairs are provided per lane to support the selected configuration.
dependencies: SerDes lane enables; board routing; partner capability
Remarks: Plan PCB routing for final lane width and avoid attempts at bifurcation.

feature_name: Multi-domain clocks and conventional reset support
description: Multiple functional and SerDes clock domains exist within the subsystem. The PHY generates required functional clocks from the supplied differential reference. The design supports conventional PCIe reset mechanisms including cold and warm reset. Reset in the block diagram corresponds to hardware-asserted resets. Clock stabilization must precede reset deassertion for deterministic bring-up.
dependencies: Reference clock source; reset controller; clock trees
Remarks: Sequence reference clock, power, and resets per timing recommendations before enabling link.

feature_name: Integrated interrupt architecture (INTx/MSI/error/PM/reset)
description: The module can generate up to 14 interrupts including combined legacy INTx, MSI, error, power management, and reset events. These sources are connected to the SoC interrupt controller. Software must acknowledge serviced interrupts by writing the corresponding vector to the EOI register. Fine-grained enable and clear registers exist for each group. Proper mapping to CPU vectors is required for responsive servicing.
dependencies: SoC interrupt controller; EOI register; enable/clear registers
Remarks: Prefer MSI where possible and clear sources before writing EOI to avoid retriggers.

feature_name: Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI) with vector banking
description: The MSI implementation provides multiple vectors organized into banks with raw and enabled status. W1S and W1C semantics are used for setting raw bits for debug and clearing enabled status respectively. Control registers exist for enable set and enable clear per bank. Specific examples include MSI2 and MSI6 register groups with documented offsets. Proper programming enables low-latency per-function interrupts without legacy signaling.
dependencies: MSI capability in config space; system MSI routing; vector table
Remarks: Allocate unique vectors and ensure ISR clears status using the documented W1C behavior.

feature_name: Legacy INTx support with disable control
description: Legacy INTA/B/C/D signaling is supported alongside MSI. Status and enable/clear registers are provided for legacy interrupt sources. The Command register field INTX_DIS allows software to disable legacy INTx message generation. This enables clean migration to MSI where supported. Retaining INTx offers compatibility with legacy hosts at the cost of potential sharing and latency.
dependencies: Command register access; interrupt controller mapping
Remarks: Disable INTx when MSI is active to prevent duplicate interrupt signaling.

feature_name: Error detection and reporting (correctable/non-fatal/fatal)
description: Device Status and Control exposes bits for Correctable, Non-fatal, and Fatal error detect and reporting enables. Software can enable reporting via CORR_ERR_RP, NFATAL_ERR_RP, and FATAL_ERR_RP and route errors with SERR_EN. UNSUP_RQ_DET flags unsupported requests for driver handling. PAR_ERR_RESP controls response to poisoned TLPs and parity-related issues. Write-one-to-clear semantics are used to acknowledge latched error status bits.
dependencies: PCIe capability space access; system error handling; logs
Remarks: Enable error reporting and implement recovery paths including link retraining or device reset.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting and ECRC generation/checking
description: The controller supports Advanced Error Reporting features including End-to-End CRC generation and checking. Control resides in the Advanced Capabilities and Control Register with enable and capability bits for ECRC. Enabling ECRC adds data integrity protection on TLPs across the fabric. Combined with advanced error logging, it aids rapid fault isolation. Configuration should align with Root Complex policy to avoid mismatches.
dependencies: AER/Advanced Capability presence; RC policy; endpoint support
Remarks: Enable ECRC only when all devices in the path advertise support to prevent malformed TLP handling.

feature_name: Payload and read request size configuration
description: Maximum Payload Size and Maximum Read Request Size are programmable to tune throughput and latency. These fields must be set within the constraints negotiated with the link partner and platform. Larger payloads can improve efficiency while potentially increasing latency for small transfers. Smaller read request sizes can reduce completion latency under contention. The driver should adjust these values post-enumeration based on system policy.
dependencies: Negotiated capabilities; RC policy; memory subsystem limits
Remarks: Validate end-to-end performance and adjust sizes conservatively on congested fabrics.

feature_name: Ordering and cache policy controls (Relaxed/No Snoop/Extended Tag/Phantom)
description: The controller supports enabling Relaxed Ordering to allow reordering where permitted. No Snoop can be asserted to bypass cache coherency where system policy allows. Extended Tag Field increases the number of outstanding transactions to raise concurrency. Phantom Function enable is provided per PCIe specification for advanced function addressing. These options should be applied only when all endpoints and the RC support them.
dependencies: System coherency model; RC and endpoint capabilities
Remarks: Audit memory ordering requirements carefully before enabling to avoid data hazards.

feature_name: BAR configuration (32/64-bit, prefetchable, memory vs I/O)
description: BAR registers support 32-bit or 64-bit memory mappings and mark regions as prefetchable or non-prefetchable. MEM_SPACE selects Memory versus I/O BAR semantics, but I/O BARs are not supported on this PCIe subsystem. Associated MASK registers define implemented size and writable address bits. Fields are writable from the internal bus interface for flexibility during initialization. Proper BAR setup is foundational for successful enumeration and access.
dependencies: BAR and MASK registers; host enumeration; internal bus access
Remarks: Use prefetchable memory for side-effect-free regions and align sizes to power-of-two boundaries.

feature_name: Device power management states (D0/D1/D2/D3hot/D3cold)
description: The PCIe function supports PCI-compatible power states with required D0 and D3 support. D1 and D2 are optional light and deeper sleep states primarily driven by software policy. In D1/D2 the device limits initiated TLPs and only accepts configuration and message requests. D3hot retains power and clock for software-driven return to D0, while D3cold is a full power-off requiring re-initialization. Drivers must quiesce outstanding transactions and save state prior to transitions.
dependencies: System PM framework; clock availability; reset behavior
Remarks: Gate transitions to avoid mid-transaction loss and restore configuration on resume.

feature_name: ASPM link states L0s/L1 (L2 not supported, L3 supported)
description: The link supports Active State Power Management transitions into L0s and L1 for autonomous energy savings. L2 is not supported by the subsystem, while L3 power-off is supported by default. ASPM transitions can be enabled via configuration space to select L0s only or both L0s and L1. Hardware manages transitions among allowed states once enabled. Software should coordinate policy with the Root Complex to balance power and latency.
dependencies: ASPM control bits; RC policy; partner device support
Remarks: Enable ASPM only when both ends advertise support and latency budget allows.

feature_name: Software-driven link state transitions (L1 and L2/L3 Ready)
description: Software can induce entry to L1 via device power state changes such as D1/D2/D3hot. From L1 the link can transition to L2/L3 Ready as a precursor to deep power-down states. L2 retains auxiliary power for wake signaling while most logic is unpowered. L3 is full power-off with no communication and requires clock re-establishment and fundamental reset for recovery. Correct sequencing ensures deterministic exit and re-initialization.
dependencies: PM registers; wake signaling; reset and refclock control
Remarks: Coordinate with system PM to preserve wake paths and avoid unintended link drops.

feature_name: SerDes PLL controls (bandwidth, sleep, VCO range, DIVCLK)
description: The SerDes exposes PLL control including selectable jitter bandwidth modes for different clocking topologies. A SLEEPPLL control places the PLL in a low-power sleep state when asserted. VRANGE selects the VCO operating range to match the target data rate and reference. ENDIVCLK enables a divided PLL clock output for observation or auxiliary use. Proper selection reduces jitter transfer and maintains standards compliance.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2-5 (PLL BW, SLEEPPLL, VRANGE, ENDIVCLK)
dependencies: SerDes control registers; reference clock quality; board PLL architecture
Remarks: Choose bandwidth per clock source cleanliness and avoid dynamic changes during active traffic.

feature_name: PHY power/ground/termination requirements
description: The PHY requires several dedicated power supplies, grounds, and proper termination networks. Correct power-up sequencing and decoupling are essential for analog performance. Termination values must meet PCIe electrical specifications. These provisions influence eye quality and link stability. Board design must follow device recommendations for rail noise and impedance control.
dependencies: Board power rails; terminations; decoupling network
Remarks: Adhere to layout and power integrity guidelines from the device datasheet and PCIe SIG.

feature_name: Differential data lines per lane (TX/RX pairs)
description: Each configured lane provides a differential transmit pair and a differential receive pair. Proper routing with controlled impedance and minimal skew is required for compliance. Lane count selection determines the number of active pairs. Signal integrity directly impacts BER and training robustness. Maintain short stubs and matched lengths across pairs.
dependencies: PCB layout; connectors; impedance control
Remarks: Follow PCIe routing rules for pair spacing, reference planes, and via design.

feature_name: Non-multiplexed functional pins and bootstrap latching
description: PCIESS functional pins are not shared with other signals to simplify integration. Only the module configuration bits PCIESSMODE[1:0] and PCIESSEN are latched at power-up via GPIO/Timer pins. This allows defining default mode and initial power domain state. Pin multiplexing does not affect the high-speed lane pins. Proper external pulls ensure deterministic boot configuration.
dependencies: GPIO/Timer strap pins; external pull resistors
Remarks: Verify strap polarities and timing so mode and power domain are latched as intended.

feature_name: EOI-based interrupt acknowledgment requirement
description: After servicing an interrupt source the software must write the corresponding vector to the End-Of-Interrupt register. This action informs the interrupt controller to clear and accept subsequent events. Failure to write EOI can leave interrupts pending and block new ones. The mechanism applies across MSI and legacy generated events routed through the controller. Correct ISR tail handling ensures timely unmasking.
dependencies: Interrupt controller EOI register; ISR sequence
Remarks: Place EOI at the end of ISR after clearing peripheral status to avoid spurious retriggers.

feature_name: Command register: bus mastering and memory space enable
description: The Command register controls enabling Bus Mastering and Memory Space decoding required for normal operation. IO Space enable is not supported in this subsystem and remains cleared. SERR_EN in the same register enables generation of PCIe error messages toward the Root Complex. Properly sequencing these enables after BAR and translation setup avoids unintended accesses. These settings are foundational to device participation on the bus.
dependencies: PCI configuration space access; RC enumeration
Remarks: Set BUS_MS and MEM_SP only after BAR sizing/programming and translation entries are valid.

feature_name: Power management and reset event interrupts
description: Dedicated interrupts indicate power management messages and reset-related link events. Examples include PM turn-off (EP), PM acknowledgment (RC), PM event notifications (RC), and link reset or link down requests (RC). These events are part of the integrated interrupt scheme and aid orderly power transitions. Correct handling ensures graceful quiesce and resume. Drivers should map and prioritize them according to system PM policy.
dependencies: PM capability registers; interrupt controller mapping
Remarks: Coordinate PM interrupts with device state machines to avoid race conditions during transitions.
